coredata.dc:title: Turn-on fluorescence sensor for glutathione in aqueous solutions using carbon dots–MnO2 nanocomposites
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Chunlei
Deng, Wenping
Liu, Haiyun
Ge, Shenguang
Yan, Mei
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescence sensor based on carbon dots–MnO2 nanocomposites was fabricated successfully, which can detect glutathione (GSH) in aqueous solutions succinctly, rapidly and selectively. The carbon dots (CDs), which were synthesized by using organosilane as a coordinating solvent, had a highly luminescent quantum yield. The nanoflower-like MnO2 had an enormous specific surface area, which could react with more CDs. Subsequently, CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites were synthesized through a facile one-step method. As a result of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from CDs to the MnO2, the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by MnO2. However, when GSH was introduced into the system, the quenched fluorescence could be restored because MnO2 was reduced to Mn2+ by GSH, which led to the elimination of FRET. Compared with other electrolytes and biomolecules, we find that the chemical response of the CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites exhibited good selectivity toward GSH. Under the optimal conditions, the proposed immunosensor was successfully performed with a linear range of 0.03–974.1μmolL−1, with a detection limit of 0.015μmolL−1. In addition, the proposed fluorescence sensor has several merits, such as low cost, good selectivity, great biocompatibility and chemical turn-on fluorescence response.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots–MnO2 nanocomposites
Fluorescence
Glutathione
Fluorescence sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The characterization of CDs, MnO2 and CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites
Fluorescence sensing of GSH based on the CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites
Selectivity of CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites based turn-on fluorescence sensor toward GSH

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites for sensing of GSH.

(A) UV–vis absorption (a) and fluorescence spectra (b) of CDs solution. Inset shows the color change of CDs solution without and with UV irradiation. (B) Spectral overlap showing the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanoparticles (a) and the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs (b). (C) Representative TEM images of nanoflower-like MnO2 nanoparticles. (D) EDS of MnO2 nanoparticles.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs (a) and CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites (b). (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (a) and CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites (b). (C) Fluorescence spectra of CDs–MnO2 prepared by different concentrations of MnO2 (a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h represent 0, 0.0051, 0.0102, 0.0153, 0.0204, 0.0256, 0.0307, 0.0358mM respectively) at excitation wavelength of 400nm. (D) The liner relationship of MnO2 concentration and nanocomposite fluorescence intensity.

(A) Fluorescence response of CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites in the presence of GSH (1mM) (a) and in the absence of GSH (b), as a function of time. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites in the presence of different concentrations of GSH (0–2mM). The inset shows plot of the fluorescence intensity versus GSH concentration (0–2mM).

Fluorescence response of the CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites toward (A) GSH, different electrolytes and biomolecules (B) NADPH, NADH, Vitamin C, Vitamin E (50μM for each) and GSH. F and F 0 represent the fluorescence intensity in the presence of the target (GSH) and the response of water, respectively.

head.ce:title: Turn-on fluorescence sensor for glutathione in aqueous solutions using carbon dots–MnO2 nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescence sensor based on carbon dots–MnO2 nanocomposites was fabricated successfully, which can detect glutathione (GSH) in aqueous solutions succinctly, rapidly and selectively. The carbon dots (CDs), which were synthesized by using organosilane as a coordinating solvent, had a highly luminescent quantum yield. The nanoflower-like MnO2 had an enormous specific surface area, which could react with more CDs. Subsequently, CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites were synthesized through a facile one-step method. As a result of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from CDs to the MnO2, the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by MnO2. However, when GSH was introduced into the system, the quenched fluorescence could be restored because MnO2 was reduced to Mn2+ by GSH, which led to the elimination of FRET. Compared with other electrolytes and biomolecules, we find that the chemical response of the CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites exhibited good selectivity toward GSH. Under the optimal conditions, the proposed immunosensor was successfully performed with a linear range of 0.03–974.1μmolL−1, with a detection limit of 0.015μmolL−1. In addition, the proposed fluorescence sensor has several merits, such as low cost, good selectivity, great biocompatibility and chemical turn-on fluorescence response.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (γ-l-glutamyl-l-cysteinylglycine) is a special water-soluble peptide with the most prevalent low-molecular weight thiol in mammalian cells, which is synthesized endogenously from the precursor amino acids l-cysteine, l-glutamic acid, and glycine [1,2]. Under normal circumstances, this tripeptide can be found in oxidized disulfide (GSSG) or reduced thiol (GSH) states and the major existing form is the reduced form (GSH). Glutathione is used in vivo almost exclusively in the reduced state hence the importance of a high GSH/GSSG ratio in the cell can never be ignored [3]. Recent studies have demonstrated that GSH have played a more and more important role in a variety of molecular reactions and biological applications in many cellular processes, including amino acid transportation, detoxification of xenobiotics, maintenance of protein redox state, neuromodulation and neurotransmission [4]. What's more, the level of GSH in tissues is related to a variety of diseases, such as AIDS, Alzheimer disease, alcoholic liver disease, cardiovascular disease, diabetes mellitus, and cancer [5–10]. Because of the biological importance of GSH, there are numbers of approaches to quantify or monitor the changes of GSH in samples, including high-performance liquid chromatography [11], electrochemistry [12], electrogenerated chemiluminescence [13], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [14], mass spectrometry [15] and fluorescence spectroscopy [16]. Compared with other analytical methods, fluorescence spectroscopy has significant advantages for its high sensitivity, simplicity, and nondestructive properties [17]. Therefore, we adopt the method of fluorescence spectroscopy for the GSH detection, which may be a valuable research.

Fluorescent probes have been recognized as the most efficient molecular tools that can help monitor and visualize trace amounts of samples in live cells or tissues because of its high sensitivity and high spatiotemporal resolution [18]. Nowadays, a variety of materials, such as organic fluorophores [19], gold nanoclusters [20], quantum dots (QDs) [21] and carbon nanomaterials [22] have been used as fluorescent probes. Among them, QDs have become one of the most extensively optical sensing nanomaterials in the detection of nucleic acids, enzymes, proteins, metal ions, and other small molecules because of high emission quantum yields and size tunable emission profiles [23]. However, those popular QDs have serious toxicity even at relatively low concentration [24]. In contrast with common QDs, carbon dots (CDs) have no burden of intrinsic toxicity or elemental scarcity and do not need for stringent, intricate, tedious, costly, or inefficient preparation steps. Therefore, CDs have attracted much interest in various fields, such as biosensing and bioimaging in live cells. At the same time because of their excellent luminescence properties, CDs have been used as benign fluorescent probes [25,26]. Hence, a design of a fluorescent probe for detecting GSH based on CDs could be available.

Manganese oxide nanoparticles have high reactivity, specificity and can be used as both catalyst and reactant. Among them, manganese dioxide (MnO2) is one of the most stable manganese oxides with excellent physical and chemical properties under ambient conditions [27–30]. As a type of transition metal oxide, it has drawn an increasing amount of attention in batteries, supercapacitors and even visible light-driven catalysis [31–34]. However, it has rarely been reported in fluorescence strategy [35]. A special morphological MnO2 is of great interest because of their unique properties including exceptionally high specific surface area and unique electronic property. Among them, MnO2 nanoparticles have a broad absorption spectrum (250–550nm) [36]. The spectrum overlaps with the fluorescence excitation and/or emission spectra of most kinds of fluorescent dyes. This feature allows the occurrence of FRET [37] with MnO2 nanomaterials, of which the FRET mechanism has recently been utilized for fluorescent sensing [38]. In our work, we synthesized a nanoflower-like MnO2 which have an enormous specific surface area by a microemulsion method. The novel materials have the merits of nanoparticles, and at the same time have controllable particle size, nice particle dispersion and narrow size distribution, which have a broad application prospect. Thus, the prepared nanoflower-like MnO2 was applied to fluorescence strategy in our work.

In this paper, a turn-on fluorescence sensor for GSH in aqueous solutions was fabricated successfully. We first used a delicate one-step method to produce CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites which was used for detecting GSH rapidly and selectively in aqueous solution. The sensor we established exhibits superior selectivity for GSH chemical response parallel with other biomolecules and electrolytes, which may be promising to apply for monitoring changes of the intracellular GSH level in living cells. The nanoflower-like MnO2 nanoparticles we prepared exhibit an enormous specific surface area, hence, may combine much more CDs. The CDs are low-toxicity and eco-friendly, which have highly luminescence, benign optical properties and inherent stability. They have drawn increasing attention owing to their biocompatible and nontoxic characters, demonstrating attractive applications in biosensor and biomedical imaging. As a result of FRET from CDs to the MnO2, the formed CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites can quench the fluorescence of CDs. When GSH appeared the system, the quenched fluorescence would be restored, which could be the reason that GSH reduced the MnO2 to Mn2+. As far as we know, the CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites fluorescence sensor could be demonstrated firstly for GSH detection and its application to constitute a fluorescent method for in vivo sensing of GSH may be promising.

N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxy silane (AEAPMS) was purchased from Beijing Shenda Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Citric acid anhydrous and potassium hypermanganate (KMnO4) were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Oleic acid was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 3-Aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTS) and ethyl dimethyamino carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl) were purchased from Xiya Reagent Research Center No. 67 Chang Lin Rd, Linshu, Shandong. Rabbit liver metallothionein (MT) II was purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Glycine (Gly), d-aspartic acid (Asp), thioredoxin (Trx) (from Escherichia coli), glucose, bovine serum albumin (BSA), GSH (reduced form), glutathione reductase (GR) and other reagents of analytical reagent grade were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade reagent or the highest purity available and directly used for the following experiments without further purification and the aqueous solutions unless indicated were prepared with doubly distilled water.

The morphology and composition of the prepared materials were characterized by a QUANTA FEG 250 thermal field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, FEI Co., USA) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) equipped with an X-MAX50 X-ray energy dispersive spectrometer (Oxford Co., UK), operated at 15kV. Ultraviolet-visible light (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Suzhou Instruments Mfg. Co. Ltd.) in the range of 200–800nm. Fluorescence spectra were collected using a RF-5301PC fluorophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Japan). The samples were excited at 325nm and the fluorescence emission ranged from 360nm to 600nm, in steps of 1nm. All UV–vis absorption and fluorescence measurements were measured at room temperature.

CDs were synthesized according to previous typical literature [39]. Firstly, 10mL AEAPMS were placed into a 100mL three-necked flask, and degassed with nitrogen for 5min. After that, the flask was heated and till 240°C, 0.5g citric acid anhydrous was then quickly added to the solution with vigorous stirring. The mixture was then kept at the temperature for 1min. Finally, the final products were purified by precipitation with petroleum ether for three times; about 2mL CDs were obtained.

MnO2 nanoflowers were prepared by a typical procedure [40]. In brief, 0.2500g KMnO4 was dissolved in 125mL of ultrapure water, and the mixture was stirred for about 0.5h. Subsequently, 2.5mL of oleic acid was then added, and a steady emulsion was formed. After the emulsion was maintained at room temperature for 24h, a brown-black product was collected, and washed several times with ultrapure water and alcohol to remove any possible residual reactants. Finally, the product was dried in air at 60°C for 12h.

The MnO2 nanoflowers were first reacted with aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTS) to modify their surface with NH2 groups [41]. Typically, MnO2 nanoflowers (50mg), APTS (100μL), and toluene (16mL) were added successively into a 250mL round-bottomed flask. Afterwards, the reaction mixture was stirred and refluxed at 120°C under N2 for 6h. The modified MnO2 nanoflowers were then washed with toluene, and dried in air at 60°C. 1mg modified MnO2 nanoflowers were firstly dissolved in 10mL ultrapure water by sonication. In a typical reaction, 200μL CDs solution was added to 2.5mL PBS buffer (0.1M, pH 7.0). 500μL of MnO2 solution and 3mg EDC·HCl were then added and the volume of mixture was adjusted to 10mL with ultrapure water. After that, the resulting mixture was sonicated for 30min so that the reaction could be completely finished.

The sensing solutions were prepared by mixing 100μL of CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites solutions with 100μL of GSH with different concentrations in 1.5mL centrifuge tubes at room temperature. The solutions were then diluted in 1mL in ultrapure water and mixed thoroughly in different incubation reaction time. Afterward, the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixtures were recorded with excitation at 325nm and the fluorescence intensity of CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites by GSH in different incubation time was studied.

Fig. 1 demonstrated the principle of CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites sensor for GSH. The MnO2 nanoflowers with functionalized amino groups could be easily connected with CDs via an acylation process, which had active group carboxyl on the surface. Similarly, the –NH2 modified MnO2 nanoflowers would react with CDs that were modified with –COOH. Thus, the CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites could be easily obtained and the nanocomposites are very stable. When GSH is injected into the system, MnO2 could be reduced by GSH, which lead to the inhibition of the FRET process between CDs and MnO2, so the fluorescence intensity restores. In the redox process, MnO2 was reduced to Mn2+ and GSH (via the sulfhydryl groups [–SH]) was oxidized to glutathione disulfide (GSSG) via –SH exchange [36], as shown by the equation below.
MnO2 +2GSH+2H+ →Mn2+ +GSSG+2H2O
Hence, the simple and facile CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites could be as a fluorescence sensor for rapid screening and quantification of the GSH levels (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of CDs, MnO2 and CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Optical properties of the CDs were characterized by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1A (a), CDs have an obvious moderate UV absorption at 200–400nm. Furthermore, an absorption edge extending to 400nm was only found in CDs, indicating the formation of CDs [42]. Under the excitation of 325nm, the CDs exhibit distinct fluorescence emission at 458nm (Fig. 1A (b)). The inset in Fig. 1A is the photographs of diluted solutions of CDs, which is nearly colorless (or a very slight yellow color) under visible light, while it emits intense green fluorescence under a 365nm UV light. Fig. 1B displays the fluorescence spectroscopy of CDs solutions and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanoparticles which was synthesized by microemulsion method. It can be observed that MnO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 1B (a)) have a broad absorption band from 250nm to 550nm, which matches well with the previous reported optical characteristic of MnO2 nanomaterials [36,43]. Obviously, as shown in Fig. 1B, the absorbance spectrum of MnO2 nanoparticles overlaps well with the fluorescence emission of the CDs, which leads to FRET from the CDs to the MnO2. To get a further understanding of the morphology of MnO2 nanoparticles, the TEM image is displayed in Fig. 1C. As seen from the TEM image, the MnO2 nanoparticles of 200nm in diameter have a nanoflower-like structure that formed by the self-assembly of MnO2 nanoplatelets. A typical energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS) spectrum of MnO2 products is shown in Fig. 1D, which shows that the obtained deposits are pure MnO2.

The formation of CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites is characterized by UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum. A broad absorption peak at 400nm was displayed in Fig. 2A (b), which demonstrates the characteristic of MnO2 nanomaterials. In addition, the fluorescence spectrum of CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites in Fig. 2B (b) further testify the formation of CDs–MnO2, which have an apparent extinction of fluorescence intensity than CDs. Fig. 2C demonstrates the fluorescence response of CDs modified with different concentrations of MnO2 (from 0mM to 0.04mM). As expected, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased as the MnO2 concentration increased (Fig. 2C). A good liner relationship is observed in Fig. 2D. The fitted equation is Y =−1.48781+5054.58484X with the concentration of MnO2 in the range of 0–0.04mM (R 2 =0.9968), as displayed in Fig. 2D. This is possibly because the MnO2 had larger superficial area, with the MnO2 concentration increasing, the absorption capacity to CDs became higher, which lead to the fluorescence of CDs be more heavily quenched.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of GSH based on the CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetics of the reaction between GSH and CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites was measured in a spectrofluorometer by monitoring the fluorescence recovery on the basis of time. Fig. 3A shows that the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually increases with the reaction time. The whole approach reached equilibrium at about 3min, which displays a rapid react process at room temperature. We select 3min as incubation time for sensitive measurement of GSH in the subsequent experiment.

We researched the fluorescence response of CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites after incubation 3min with different GSH concentrations in aqueous solutions for the purpose of studying the applicability of the fluorescence sensor for GSH determination. With the increase of GSH concentration from 0mM to 2mM, the fluorescence of the CDs restored gradually and the sensing response reached the maximum finally, which showed no further more fluorescent enhancement. The inset in Fig. 3B shows the plot of fluorescence enhancement (F–F 0) against the concentration of GSH The equation of fitted curve is Y =171.3–242.8/(1+exp((X −316.3)/432.9)). The fitted curve could be used for the quantification of GSH with a correlation coefficient of 0.9975, and the detection limit could reach as low as 0.015μM based on the definition of 3 times the deviation of the blank signal (3σ). The liner fitted equation is Y =9.08669+0.12413X, R 2 =0.9957. We attain the final result that the linear range is 0.03–974.1μmolL−1 by calculation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites based turn-on fluorescence sensor toward GSH
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is a critical parameter to evaluate the performance of a fluorescence immunosensor. The influence of some electrolytes and biomolecules (amino acids, proteins, etc.) was added into aqueous solutions to evaluate the selectivity of CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites for GSH and the experimental results was shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4A demonstrates that CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites exhibit a higher fluorescence response toward GSH concentrations from 200μM to 500μM. However, we do not observe a noticeable fluorescence response changes with other electrolytes and biomolecules. In addition, protein thiols at the intracellular or higher concentration [44] produced a very low fluorescence response, which could be owing to the steric factor between nanocomposites and protein (including GR, Trx, MT in Fig. 4A). What's more, some nonthiol reducing agents (Vitamin E, Vitamin C, NADPH, NADH in Fig. 4B) at the concentrations according to the intracellular concentrations or slightly more than intracellular concentrations [44,45] was also detected. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 4B. On the average, Fig. 4 shows that CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites display a highly selective fluorescence response toward GSH over other nontarget samples.

In this study, a simple and efficient CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites were prepared by a facile one-step acylation process route combined with a mild ultrasonic method. Because of FRET from CDs to the MnO2, the fluorescence of the formed CDs can be quenched by MnO2. When GSH was introduced into the system, the quenched fluorescence could be restored. Therefore, this fluorescence turn-on nanoprobe has been successfully applied to monitor changes of the GSH level in solution and is promising to be applied to monitor intracellular GSH level in living cells. Compared with other electrolytes and biomolecules, the chemical response of the CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites exhibits good selectivity toward GSH. In the meanwhile, the CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites have several appealing features, including low cost, easy preparation, low biotoxicity, nice biocompatibility and turn-on fluorescence response. As a result, the immunoassay exhibited high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, low detection limit and accepted precision on the detection of GSH. Therefore, the novel fluorescence sensor may have a promising applicability for quantitative GSH detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51273084, 51473067, 21277058), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2012BZ002), Technology Development Plan of Shandong Province, China (Grant No. 2012GGB01181).

Fluorescent probes for sensing and imaging

Gold nanocluster-based fluorescent probes for near-infrared and turning-on sensing of glutathione in living cells
Highly selective detection of glutathione using quantum-dots-based OFF-ON fluorescent probes
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Label-free fluorescent detection of protein kinase activity based on the aggregation behavior of unmodified quantum dots
Probing the cytotoxicity of semiconductor quantum dots
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Thermochemistry and oxidation potentials of manganese and its compounds
Growth of manganese oxide nanoflowers on vertically-aligned carbon nanotube arrays for high-rate electrochemical capacitive energy storage
Facile controlled synthesis of MnO2 nanostructures of novel shapes and their application in batteries
Alcohol-assisted room temperature synthesis of different nanostructured manganese oxides and their pseudocapacitance properties in neutral electrolyte
Mesoporous hydrous manganese dioxide nanowall arrays with large lithium ion energy storage capacities
Manganese oxide-based materials as electrochemical supercapacitor electrodes
Solar driven water oxidation by a bioinspired manganese molecular catalyst
Nanostructured alkaline-cation-containing δ-MnO2 for photocatalytic water oxidation
Single-layer MnO2 nanosheets suppressed fluorescence of 7-hydroxycoumarin: mechanistic study and application for sensitive sensing of ascorbic acid in vivo
Intracellular glutathione detection using MnO2-nanosheet modified upconversion nanoparticles
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer between quantum dots and graphene oxide for sensing biomolecules
Facile synthesis of graphene-wrapped honeycomb MnO2 nanospheres and their application in supercapacitors
Fabrication of graphene-encapsulated oxide nanoparticles: towards high-performance anode materials for lithium storage
One step synthesis of C-dots by microwave mediated caramelization of poly(ethylene glycol)
Synthesizing MnO2 nanosheets from graphene oxide templates for high performance pseudosupercapacitors
A rhodamine-based fluorescent probe containing a Se–N bond for detecting thiols and its application in living cells
Imaging cytosolic NADH-NAD+ redox state with a genetically encoded fluorescent biosensor

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chunlei Yang studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.
Wenping Deng studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.

Haiyun Liu received his PhD degree in Chemical Engineering and Materials Science from Shandong Normal University in 2014. He then worked in University of Jinan, where currently he is a lecturer of chemistry. His current scientific interests are focused on nucleic acid DNA-based sensor, bio-imaging and biochemical analysis.

Shenguang Ge received his PhD degree in Chemistry and Chemical Engineering in 2013 from Shandong University, completed his master degree studies in University of Jinan in 2006. He joined University of Jinan, where currently he is a lecturer of chemistry. His research interests are in the area of biosensor and chemsensor.

Mei Yan received her BSc in Applied Chemistry from University of Jinan in 1999, and obtained her PhD in 2005 from Institute of Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences. She then joined University of Jinan, as an associate professor working on the synthesis and performance of advanced functional materials.
